alienSedition.title: The Alien and Sedition Acts: Fear as Policy, Power as Habit
alienSedition.subtitle: Fear as Policy, Power as Habit
alienSedition.summary: An examination of the four 1798 statutes that criminalized dissent in the early American republic — and the one that quietly survived into the twenty-first century.
alienSedition.abstract: In the summer of 1798, a young republic under the pressure of an undeclared naval war with France passed four statutes aimed at political stability as Federalists defined it. This essay traces how the Quasi-War climate of suspicion produced the Naturalization, Alien Friends, Sedition, and Alien Enemies Acts; how enforcement targeted opposition editors, congressmen, and veterans; how three of the four Acts were repudiated by the electorate of 1800; and how the fourth — the Alien Enemies Act — was preserved, amended, and reactivated across two centuries of American wartime governance.
